We show that prompt engineering that combines few-shot examples and diverse input formats, such as image embeddings, significantly improves LLM-human alignment, and additional filtering by the confidence score of the LLM pushes the alignment to human-human levels. Furthermore, we demonstrate that carefully trained VMs can achieve VM-human alignment at a level comparable to that between human annotators. Our results suggest that AI can feasibly serve as a scalable proxy for human labelers.

AI.tldr: Large language models and vision models can effectively serve as scalable proxies for human judgment in network visualization preferences, achieving alignment levels comparable to human-human agreement when properly engineered and filtered.
AI.motivation: Traditional network visualization relies on heuristic metrics (like stress) but no single metric consistently produces effective layouts. While learning from human preferences could create better generative models, obtaining human labels at scale is costly and time-consuming, limiting practical application.
AI.method: Conducted user study with 27 participants to curate human preference labels; used this data to bootstrap LLM/VM labelers; employed prompt engineering with few-shot examples and diverse input formats (including image embeddings); implemented confidence score filtering; trained vision models on human preference data.
AI.result: Combined few-shot examples with diverse input formats significantly improves LLM-human alignment; confidence score filtering pushes alignment to human-human levels; carefully trained vision models achieve VM-human alignment comparable to human-human agreement.
AI.conclusion: AI models (LLMs and VMs) can feasibly serve as scalable proxies for human labelers in network visualization preference tasks, enabling data-driven approaches that were previously limited by the cost and time of human annotation.

title: TABQAWORLD: Optimizing Multimodal Reasoning for Multi-Turn Table Question Answering
summary: Multimodal reasoning has emerged as a powerful framework for enhancing reasoning capabilities of reasoning models. While multi-turn table reasoning methods have improved reasoning accuracy through tool use and reward modeling, they rely on fixed text serialization for table state readouts. This introduces representation errors in table encoding that significantly accumulate over multiple turns. Such accumulation is alleviated by tabular grounding methods in the expense of inference compute and cost, rendering real world deployment impractical. To address this, we introduce TABQAWORLD, a table reasoning framework that jointly optimizes tabular action through representation and estimation. For representation, TABQAWORLD employs an action-conditioned multimodal selection policy, which dynamically switches between visual and textual representations to maximize table state readout reliability. For estimation, TABQAWORLD optimizes stepwise reasoning trajectory through table metadata including dimension, data types and key values, safely planning trajectory and compressing low-complexity actions to reduce conversation turns and latency. Designed as a training-free framework, empirical evaluations show that TABQAWORLD achieves state-of-the-art performance with 4.87% accuracy improvements over baselines, with 5.42% accuracy gain and 33.35% inference latency reduction over static settings, establishing a new standard for reliable and efficient table reasoning.

AI.tldr: TABQAWORLD is a training-free table reasoning framework that jointly optimizes tabular action through dynamic multimodal representation switching and metadata-based trajectory planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance with significant accuracy improvements and latency reduction.
AI.motivation: Current multi-turn table reasoning methods rely on fixed text serialization for table state readouts, causing representation errors that accumulate over multiple turns. Tabular grounding methods alleviate this but are impractical for real-world deployment due to high inference compute and cost.
AI.method: TABQAWORLD introduces two key components: 1) Action-conditioned multimodal selection policy that dynamically switches between visual and textual representations to maximize table state readout reliability, and 2) Metadata-based trajectory optimization using table dimensions, data types, and key values to safely plan reasoning trajectories and compress low-complexity actions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show TABQAWORLD achieves state-of-the-art performance with 4.87% accuracy improvements over baselines, including 5.42% accuracy gain and 33.35% inference latency reduction compared to static representation settings.
AI.conclusion: TABQAWORLD establishes a new standard for reliable and efficient table reasoning by addressing representation error accumulation through joint optimization of representation and estimation, while being training-free and practical for real-world deployment.

title: Agents for Agents: An Interrogator-Based Secure Framework for Autonomous Internet of Underwater Things
comment: This paper was presented in ICETAS 2026 in Bahrain
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) and sensor nodes increasingly support decentralized sensing and coordination in the Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT), yet most deployments rely on static trust once authentication is established, leaving long-duration missions vulnerable to compromised or behaviorally deviating agents. In this paper, an interrogator based structure is presented that incorporates the idea of behavioral trust monitoring into underwater multi-agent operation without interfering with autonomy. Privileged interrogator module is a passive communication metadata analyzer that uses a lightweight transformer model to calculate dynamic trust scores, which are used to authorize the forwarding of mission critical data. Suspicious agents cause proportional monitoring and conditional restrictions, which allow fast containment and maintain network continuity. The evidence of trust is stored in a permissioned blockchain consortium which offers identity management which is not tampered and is decentralized without causing the overhead of public consensus mechanisms. Simulation based analysis shows that the evaluation of the result compares to a relative improvement of 21.7% in the detection accuracy compared to the static trust baselines with limited energy overhead. These findings suggest that behavior driven validation has the capability of reinforcing underwater coordination without compromising scalability and deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes an interrogator-based trust monitoring system for underwater multi-agent networks using lightweight transformers and blockchain for dynamic trust scoring to detect compromised agents.
AI.motivation: Current underwater IoT deployments rely on static trust after authentication, leaving long-duration missions vulnerable to compromised or behaviorally deviating agents that could undermine mission-critical operations.
AI.method: Uses privileged interrogator modules as passive communication metadata analyzers with lightweight transformer models to calculate dynamic trust scores. Trust evidence is stored in a permissioned blockchain consortium for tamper-proof decentralized identity management without public consensus overhead.
AI.result: Simulation analysis shows 21.7% improvement in detection accuracy compared to static trust baselines with limited energy overhead, enabling fast containment of suspicious agents while maintaining network continuity.
AI.conclusion: Behavior-driven trust validation can reinforce underwater multi-agent coordination without compromising scalability and deployment, addressing security vulnerabilities in long-duration underwater missions.

title: Adaptive Threshold-Driven Continuous Greedy Method for Scalable Submodular Optimization
summary: Submodular maximization under matroid constraints is a fundamental problem in combinatorial optimization with applications in sensing, data summarization, active learning, and resource allocation. While the Sequential Greedy (SG) algorithm achieves only a $\frac{1}{2}$-approximation due to irrevocable selections, Continuous Greedy (CG) attains the optimal $\bigl(1-\frac{1}{e}\bigr)$-approximation via the multilinear relaxation, at the cost of a progressively dense decision vector that forces agents to exchange feature embeddings for nearly every ground-set element. We propose \textit{ATCG} (\underline{A}daptive \underline{T}hresholded \underline{C}ontinuous \underline{G}reedy), which gates gradient evaluations behind a per-partition progress ratio $η_i$, expanding each agent's active set only when current candidates fail to capture sufficient marginal gain, thereby directly bounding which feature embeddings are ever transmitted. Theoretical analysis establishes a curvature-aware approximation guarantee with effective factor $τ_{\mathrm{eff}}=\max\{τ,1-c\}$, interpolating between the threshold-based guarantee and the low-curvature regime where \textit{ATCG} recovers the performance of CG. Experiments on a class-balanced prototype selection problem over a subset of the CIFAR-10 animal dataset show that \textit{ATCG} achieves objective values comparable to those of the full CG method while substantially reducing communication overhead through adaptive active-set expansion.

AI.tldr: ATCG introduces an adaptive thresholded continuous greedy algorithm for submodular maximization under matroid constraints that achieves near-optimal approximation while dramatically reducing communication overhead compared to standard Continuous Greedy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the communication overhead problem in Continuous Greedy (CG) algorithm for distributed submodular maximization. While CG achieves optimal (1-1/e) approximation, it requires agents to exchange feature embeddings for nearly every ground-set element due to its progressively dense decision vector, leading to high communication costs.
AI.method: The paper proposes ATCG (Adaptive Thresholded Continuous Greedy) which gates gradient evaluations behind per-partition progress ratios. Each agent expands its active set only when current candidates fail to capture sufficient marginal gain. This approach directly bounds which feature embeddings are transmitted, reducing communication while maintaining approximation quality.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows ATCG achieves a curvature-aware approximation guarantee with effective factor interpolating between threshold-based guarantee and the low-curvature regime where it recovers CG performance. Experiments on class-balanced prototype selection with CIFAR-10 animal dataset show ATCG achieves comparable objective values to full CG while substantially reducing communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: ATCG successfully addresses the communication-efficiency problem in distributed submodular maximization under matroid constraints, providing a practical algorithm that maintains near-optimal approximation while dramatically reducing communication requirements compared to traditional approaches.

title: Contextual Control without Memory Growth in a Context-Switching Task
summary: Context-dependent sequential decision making is commonly addressed either by providing context explicitly as an input or by increasing recurrent memory so that contextual information can be represented internally. We study a third alternative: realizing contextual dependence by intervening on a shared recurrent latent state, without enlarging recurrent dimensionality. To this end, we introduce an intervention-based recurrent architecture in which a recurrent core first constructs a shared pre-intervention latent state, and context then acts through an additive, context-indexed operator. We evaluate this idea on a context-switching sequential decision task under partial observability. We compare three model families: a label-assisted baseline with direct context access, a memory baseline with enlarged recurrent state, and the proposed intervention model, which uses no direct context input to the recurrent core and no memory growth. On the main benchmark, the intervention model performs strongly without additional recurrent dimensions. We also evaluate the models using the conditional mutual information (I(C;O | S)) as a theorem-motivated operational probe of contextual dependence at fixed latent state. For task-relevant phase-1 outcomes, the intervention model exhibits positive conditional contextual information. Together, these results suggest that intervention on a shared recurrent state provides a viable alternative to recurrent memory growth for contextual control in this setting.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an intervention-based recurrent architecture for context-dependent sequential decision making that modifies shared recurrent latent states via additive context-specific operators, avoiding both explicit context input and recurrent memory growth.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to context-dependent sequential decision making either provide context explicitly as input or increase recurrent memory to internally represent context. The authors want to explore a third alternative that avoids both explicit context input and memory growth while still achieving contextual control.
AI.method: The authors introduce an intervention-based recurrent architecture where a recurrent core first constructs a shared pre-intervention latent state, and then context acts through an additive, context-indexed operator. This modifies the latent state without enlarging recurrent dimensionality. They test this on a context-switching sequential decision task under partial observability, comparing three models: label-assisted baseline (direct context access), memory baseline (enlarged recurrent state), and the intervention model.
AI.result: The intervention model performs strongly on the main benchmark without additional recurrent dimensions. Using conditional mutual information (I(C;O|S)) as a probe of contextual dependence, the intervention model exhibits positive conditional contextual information for task-relevant phase-1 outcomes, indicating effective contextual dependence at fixed latent state.
AI.conclusion: Intervention on a shared recurrent state provides a viable alternative to recurrent memory growth for contextual control in sequential decision making under partial observability, achieving strong performance without explicit context input or increased dimensionality.

title: Decentralized Ergodic Coverage Control in Unknown Time-Varying Environments
summary: A key challenge in disaster response is maintaining situational awareness of an evolving landscape, which requires balancing exploration of unobserved regions with sustained monitoring of changing Regions of Interest (ROIs). Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have emerged as an effective response tool, particularly in applications like environmental monitoring and search-and-rescue, due to their ability to provide aerial coverage, withstand hazardous conditions, and navigate quickly and flexibly. However, efficient and adaptable multi-robot coverage with limited sensing in disaster settings and evolving time-varying information maps remains a significant challenge, necessitating better methods for UAVs to continuously adapt their trajectories in response to changes. In this paper, we propose a decentralized multi-agent coverage framework that serves as a high-level planning strategy for adaptive coverage in unknown, time-varying environments under partial observability. Each agent computes an adaptive ergodic policy, implemented via a Markov-chain transition model, that tracks a continuously updated belief over the underlying importance map. Gaussian Processes are used to perform those online belief updates. The resulting policy drives agents to spend time in ROIs proportional to their estimated importance, while preserving sufficient exploration to detect and adapt to time-varying environmental changes. Unlike existing approaches that assume known importance maps, require centralized coordination, or assume a static environment, our framework addresses the combined challenges of unknown, time-varying distributions in a more realistic decentralized and partially observable setting. We compare against alternative coverage strategies and analyze our method's response to simulated disaster evolution, highlighting its improved adaptability and transient performance in dynamic scenarios.

AI.tldr: A decentralized multi-agent coverage framework using adaptive ergodic policies with Gaussian Processes for belief updates to balance exploration and monitoring in unknown, time-varying disaster environments.
AI.motivation: Efficient multi-robot coverage in disaster response requires balancing exploration of unobserved regions with sustained monitoring of changing Regions of Interest. Current approaches fail to address combined challenges of unknown, time-varying distributions in decentralized, partially observable settings.
AI.method: Decentralized multi-agent framework where each agent computes adaptive ergodic policies via Markov-chain transition models. Policies track continuously updated belief over importance maps using Gaussian Processes for online belief updates, driving agents to spend time proportional to estimated ROI importance while preserving exploration.
AI.result: The framework shows improved adaptability and transient performance in dynamic scenarios compared to alternative coverage strategies, effectively responding to simulated disaster evolution while operating in decentralized, partially observable settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the combined challenges of unknown, time-varying importance maps in disaster response settings, offering a practical solution for decentralized multi-UAV coverage that adapts to environmental changes without centralized coordination.

title: Adversarial Robustness of Deep State Space Models for Forecasting
summary: State-space model (SSM) for time-series forecasting have demonstrated strong empirical performance on benchmark datasets, yet their robustness under adversarial perturbations is poorly understood. We address this gap through a control-theoretic lens, focusing on the recently proposed Spacetime SSM forecaster. We first establish that the decoder-only Spacetime architecture can represent the optimal Kalman predictor when the underlying data-generating process is autoregressive - a property no other SSM possesses. Building on this, we formulate robust forecaster design as a Stackelberg game against worst-case stealthy adversaries constrained by a detection budget, and solve it via adversarial training. We derive closed-form bounds on adversarial forecasting error that expose how open-loop instability, closed-loop instability, and decoder state dimension each amplify vulnerability - offering actionable principles towards robust forecaster design. Finally, we show that even adversaries with no access to the forecaster can nonetheless construct effective attacks by exploiting the model's locally linear input-output behavior, bypassing gradient computations entirely. Experiments on the Monash benchmark datasets highlight that model-free attacks, without any gradient computation, can cause at least 33% more error than projected gradient descent with a small step size.

AI.tldr: Spacetime SSM time-series forecasting models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and this paper analyzes their robustness from a control-theoretic perspective, showing that even gradient-free attacks can significantly degrade performance.
AI.motivation: State-space models (SSMs) show strong performance in time-series forecasting but their robustness to adversarial perturbations is poorly understood, creating a critical gap in understanding their real-world reliability and security.
AI.method: The authors use a control-theoretic approach, first proving that Spacetime SSM can represent optimal Kalman predictors for autoregressive processes. They formulate robust forecasting as a Stackelberg game against stealthy adversaries, use adversarial training, derive closed-form error bounds, and investigate gradient-free attacks exploiting linear input-output behavior.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that open-loop instability, closed-loop instability, and decoder state dimension amplify adversarial vulnerability. Gradient-free attacks exploiting local linearity can cause at least 33% more error than projected gradient descent attacks on Monash benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: SSM-based time-series forecasters are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, including gradient-free methods, and the control-theoretic analysis provides actionable principles for designing more robust forecasting systems that must consider adversarial robustness alongside predictive accuracy.

title: Beyond Predefined Schemas: TRACE-KG for Context-Enriched Knowledge Graphs from Complex Documents
summary: Knowledge graph construction typically relies either on predefined ontologies or on schema-free extraction. Ontology-driven pipelines enforce consistent typing but require costly schema design and maintenance, whereas schema-free methods often produce fragmented graphs with weak global organization, especially in long technical documents with dense, context-dependent information. We propose TRACE-KG (Text-dRiven schemA for Context-Enriched Knowledge Graphs), a multimodal framework that jointly constructs a context-enriched knowledge graph and an induced schema without assuming a predefined ontology. TRACE-KG captures conditional relations through structured qualifiers and organizes entities and relations using a data-driven schema that serves as a reusable semantic scaffold while preserving full traceability to the source evidence. Experiments show that TRACE-KG produces structurally coherent, traceable knowledge graphs and offers a practical alternative to both ontology-driven and schema-free construction pipelines.

AI.tldr: TRACE-KG is a multimodal framework that jointly constructs knowledge graphs with induced schemas without predefined ontologies, capturing conditional relations and preserving source evidence traceability.
AI.motivation: Current knowledge graph construction faces a trade-off: ontology-driven methods require costly schema design/maintenance but ensure consistency, while schema-free methods often produce fragmented graphs with weak organization, especially in complex technical documents.
AI.method: TRACE-KG is a multimodal framework that jointly constructs context-enriched knowledge graphs with induced schemas without assuming predefined ontologies. It captures conditional relations through structured qualifiers and organizes entities/relations using a data-driven schema that serves as a reusable semantic scaffold while preserving full traceability to source evidence.
AI.result: Experiments show that TRACE-KG produces structurally coherent, traceable knowledge graphs and offers a practical alternative to both ontology-driven and schema-free construction pipelines.
AI.conclusion: TRACE-KG successfully bridges the gap between ontology-driven and schema-free approaches by providing a framework that maintains structural coherence and traceability while eliminating the need for costly predefined ontologies, making it a viable alternative for knowledge graph construction from complex documents.

title: Statistical Model Checking of the Island Model: An Established Economic Agent-Based Model of Endogenous Growth
summary: Agent-based models (ABMs) are increasingly used to study complex economic phenomena such as endogenous growth, but their analysis typically relies on ad-hoc Monte Carlo exercises without formal statistical guarantees. We show how statistical model checking (SMC), and in particular Multi-VeStA, can automate and enrich the analysis of a seminal ABM: the Island Model of Fagiolo and Dosi, which captures the exploration-exploitation trade-off in technological search. We reproduce key stylized facts from the original model with formal confidence intervals, confirm the optimality of moderate exploration rates, and perform a counterfactual sensitivity analysis across returns to scale, skill transfer, and knowledge locality. Using MultiVeStA's built-in Welch's t-test, 6 out of 7 pairwise parameter comparisons yield statistically different growth trajectories, while the exception reveals a saturation effect in knowledge locality. Our results demonstrate that SMC offers a principled, reproducible methodology for the quantitative analysis of agent-based economic models.

AI.tldr: The paper shows how Statistical Model Checking (SMC) and Multi-VeStA can provide formal statistical analysis for agent-based economic models, using the Island Model to reproduce stylized facts with confidence intervals and validate optimal exploration rates.
AI.motivation: Agent-based models are widely used in economics but typically rely on ad-hoc Monte Carlo analysis without formal statistical guarantees, limiting their reproducibility and quantitative rigor.
AI.method: The authors apply Statistical Model Checking (SMC), specifically the Multi-VeStA tool, to analyze the Island Model of Fagiolo and Dosi, enabling formal statistical verification with confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.
AI.result: They reproduce key stylized facts with confidence intervals, confirm optimal moderate exploration rates, perform sensitivity analysis across parameters, and find that 6 out of 7 pairwise parameter comparisons show statistically different growth trajectories, with one revealing a saturation effect.
AI.conclusion: SMC offers a principled, reproducible methodology for quantitative analysis of agent-based economic models, providing formal statistical guarantees that overcome the limitations of traditional Monte Carlo approaches.

title: MetaSAEs: Joint Training with a Decomposability Penalty Produces More Atomic Sparse Autoencoder Latents
summary:
Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are increasingly used for safety-relevant applications including alignment detection and model steering. These use cases require SAE latents to be as atomic as possible. Each latent should represent a single coherent concept drawn from a single underlying representational subspace. In practice, SAE latents blend representational subspaces together. A single feature can activate across semantically distinct contexts that share no true common representation, muddying an already complex picture of model computation. We introduce a joint training objective that directly penalizes this subspace blending. A small meta SAE is trained alongside the primary SAE to sparsely reconstruct the primary SAE's decoder columns; the primary SAE is penalized whenever its decoder directions are easy to reconstruct from the meta dictionary. This occurs whenever latent directions lie in a subspace spanned by other primary directions. This creates gradient pressure toward more mutually independent decoder directions that resist sparse meta-compression.
  On GPT-2 large (layer 20), the selected configuration reduces mean $|\varphi|$ by 7.5% relative to an identical solo SAE trained on the same data. Automated interpretability (fuzzing) scores improve by 7.6%, providing external validation of the atomicity gain independent of the training and co-occurrence metrics. Reconstruction overhead is modest. Results on Gemma 2 9B are directional. On not-fully-converged SAEs, the same parameterization yields the best results, a $+8.6\%$ $Δ$Fuzz. Though directional, this is an encouraging sign that the method transfers to a larger model. Qualitative analysis confirms that features firing on polysemantic tokens are split into semantically distinct sub-features, each specializing in a distinct representational subspace.

AI.tldr: A method to improve sparse autoencoder (SAE) atomicity by penalizing subspace blending through joint training with a meta SAE, resulting in more coherent features that specialize in distinct representational subspaces.
AI.motivation: SAEs are used for safety-critical applications like alignment detection and model steering, which require atomic latents representing single coherent concepts. However, current SAEs blend multiple representational subspaces, causing features to activate across semantically distinct contexts and muddying model interpretation.
AI.method: Introduce a joint training objective with a small meta SAE that reconstructs the primary SAE's decoder columns sparsely. The primary SAE is penalized when its decoder directions are easily reconstructable by the meta dictionary, creating gradient pressure toward more mutually independent decoder directions that resist sparse meta-compression.
AI.result: On GPT-2 large (layer 20): 7.5% reduction in mean |φ|, 7.6% improvement in automated interpretability (fuzzing) scores. Results on Gemma 2 9B are directional but show +8.6% ΔFuzz on not-fully-converged SAEs. Qualitative analysis confirms polysemantic features are split into semantically distinct sub-features.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully reduces subspace blending in SAE latents, improving feature atomicity while maintaining modest reconstruction overhead. The approach shows promising transferability to larger models and provides a path toward more interpretable and reliable SAEs for safety applications.

title: Resource-Conscious Modeling for Next- Day Discharge Prediction Using Clinical Notes
summary: Timely discharge prediction is essential for optimizing bed turnover and resource allocation in elective spine surgery units. This study evaluates the feasibility of lightweight, fine-tuned large language models (LLMs) and traditional text-based models for predicting next-day discharge using postoperative clinical notes. We compared 13 models, including TF-IDF with XGBoost and LGBM, and compact LLMs (DistilGPT-2, Bio_ClinicalBERT) fine-tuned via LoRA. TF-IDF with LGBM achieved the best balance, with an F1-score of 0.47 for the discharge class, a recall of 0.51, and the highest AUC-ROC (0.80). While LoRA improved recall in DistilGPT2, overall transformer-based and generative models underperformed. These findings suggest interpretable, resource-efficient models may outperform compact LLMs in real-world, imbalanced clinical prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: Compact traditional models like TF-IDF with LGBM outperform fine-tuned lightweight LLMs for next-day discharge prediction in elective spine surgery using postoperative clinical notes.
AI.motivation: Timely discharge prediction is crucial for optimizing bed turnover and resource allocation in elective spine surgery units, but existing methods may be inefficient or impractical for real-world clinical settings.
AI.method: Compared 13 models including traditional text-based models (TF-IDF with XGBoost and LGBM) and lightweight large language models (DistilGPT-2, Bio_ClinicalBERT) fine-tuned using LoRA, evaluated on predicting next-day discharge from postoperative clinical notes.
AI.result: TF-IDF with LGBM achieved the best performance balance: F1-score of 0.47 for discharge class, recall of 0.51, and highest AUC-ROC (0.80). LoRA improved recall in DistilGPT2 but overall transformer-based and generative models underperformed compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable, resource-efficient traditional models may outperform compact LLMs in real-world, imbalanced clinical prediction tasks, suggesting simpler methods can be more effective than lightweight transformer architectures for this specific healthcare application.

title: Agentic Federated Learning: The Future of Distributed Training Orchestration
summary: Although Federated Learning (FL) promises privacy and distributed collaboration, its effectiveness in real-world scenarios is often hampered by the stochastic heterogeneity of clients and unpredictable system dynamics. Existing static optimization approaches fail to adapt to these fluctuations, resulting in resource underutilization and systemic bias. In this work, we propose a paradigm shift towards Agentic-FL, a framework where Language Model-based Agents (LMagents) assume autonomous orchestration roles. Unlike rigid protocols, we demonstrate how server-side agents can mitigate selection bias through contextual reasoning, while client-side agents act as local guardians, dynamically managing privacy budgets and adapting model complexity to hardware constraints. More than just resolving technical inefficiencies, this integration signals the evolution of FL towards decentralized ecosystems, where collaboration is negotiated autonomously, paving the way for future markets of incentive-based models and algorithmic justice. We discuss the reliability (hallucinations) and security challenges of this approach, outlining a roadmap for resilient multi-agent systems in federated environments.

AI.tldr: Agentic-FL framework introduces Language Model-based Agents for autonomous orchestration in federated learning, addressing client heterogeneity and system dynamics while enabling decentralized collaboration.
AI.motivation: Current federated learning approaches suffer from static optimization that fails to adapt to stochastic client heterogeneity and unpredictable system dynamics, leading to resource underutilization and systemic bias.
AI.method: Proposes Agentic-FL framework with Language Model-based Agents (LMagents) where server-side agents mitigate selection bias through contextual reasoning, and client-side agents act as local guardians managing privacy budgets and adapting model complexity to hardware constraints.
AI.result: The framework enables autonomous orchestration, dynamic adaptation to client heterogeneity and system constraints, and evolves federated learning towards decentralized ecosystems with negotiated collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Agentic-FL represents a paradigm shift towards autonomous, decentralized federated learning ecosystems, though reliability (hallucinations) and security challenges must be addressed for resilient multi-agent systems in federated environments.

title: Olmo Hybrid: From Theory to Practice and Back
summary: Recent work has demonstrated the potential of non-transformer language models, especially linear recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and hybrid models that mix recurrence and attention. Yet there is no consensus on whether the potential benefits of these new architectures justify the risk and effort of scaling them up. To address this, we provide evidence for the advantages of hybrid models over pure transformers on several fronts. First, theoretically, we show that hybrid models do not merely inherit the expressivity of transformers and linear RNNs, but can express tasks beyond both, such as code execution. Putting this theory to practice, we train Olmo Hybrid, a 7B-parameter model largely comparable to Olmo 3 7B but with the sliding window layers replaced by Gated DeltaNet layers. We show that Olmo Hybrid outperforms Olmo 3 across standard pretraining and mid-training evaluations, demonstrating the benefit of hybrid models in a controlled, large-scale setting. We find that the hybrid model scales significantly more efficiently than the transformer, explaining its higher performance. However, its unclear why greater expressivity on specific formal problems should result in better scaling or superior performance on downstream tasks unrelated to those problems. To explain this apparent gap, we return to theory and argue why increased expressivity should translate to better scaling efficiency, completing the loop. Overall, our results suggest that hybrid models mixing attention and recurrent layers are a powerful extension to the language modeling paradigm: not merely to reduce memory during inference, but as a fundamental way to obtain more expressive models that scale better during pretraining.

AI.tldr: Hybrid models mixing attention and recurrence outperform pure transformers in scaling efficiency and performance, with theoretical and empirical evidence showing greater expressivity and better pretraining scaling.
AI.motivation: To determine if the potential benefits of non-transformer architectures (like linear RNNs and hybrid models) justify scaling them up, by evaluating their advantages over pure transformers.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of hybrid models' expressivity and training Olmo Hybrid, a 7B-parameter model with Gated DeltaNet layers replacing sliding window layers, compared to Olmo 3 7B.
AI.result: Olmo Hybrid outperforms Olmo 3 across standard pretraining and mid-training evaluations, with hybrid models scaling significantly more efficiently than transformers, leading to higher performance.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid models mixing attention and recurrent layers represent a powerful extension to the language modeling paradigm, offering not just inference memory reduction but fundamentally more expressive models that scale better during pretraining.

title: BioAlchemy: Distilling Biological Literature into Reasoning-Ready Reinforcement Learning Training Data
summary: Despite the large corpus of biology training text, the impact of reasoning models on biological research generally lags behind math and coding. In this work, we show that biology questions from current large-scale reasoning datasets do not align well with modern research topic distributions in biology, and that this topic imbalance may negatively affect performance. In addition, we find that methods for extracting challenging and verifiable research problems from biology research text are a critical yet underdeveloped ingredient in applying reinforcement learning for better performance on biology research tasks. We introduce BioAlchemy, a pipeline for sourcing a diverse set of verifiable question-and-answer pairs from a scientific corpus of biology research text. We curate BioAlchemy-345K, a training dataset containing over 345K scientific reasoning problems in biology. Then, we demonstrate how aligning our dataset to the topic distribution of modern scientific biology can be used with reinforcement learning to improve reasoning performance. Finally, we present BioAlchemist-8B, which improves over its base reasoning model by 9.12% on biology benchmarks. These results demonstrate the efficacy of our approach for developing stronger scientific reasoning capabilities in biology. The BioAlchemist-8B model is available at: https://huggingface.co/BioAlchemy.

AI.tldr: A pipeline called BioAlchemy creates diverse, verifiable biology reasoning problems aligned with modern research topics, which when used with reinforcement learning improves biology reasoning performance by 9.12%.
AI.motivation: Biology reasoning models lag behind math and coding despite available training text, because current reasoning datasets don't align with modern biology research topics, creating topic imbalance that hurts performance, and methods for extracting challenging research problems from biology text are underdeveloped.
AI.method: BioAlchemy pipeline sources diverse, verifiable question-answer pairs from biology research text corpus, creating BioAlchemy-345K dataset with over 345K scientific reasoning problems. They align dataset to modern scientific biology topic distribution and use reinforcement learning to improve reasoning performance.
AI.result: Created BioAlchemist-8B model that improves over its base reasoning model by 9.12% on biology benchmarks, demonstrating efficacy of their approach for developing stronger scientific reasoning capabilities in biology.
AI.conclusion: The BioAlchemy approach effectively addresses the topic imbalance problem in biology reasoning datasets and demonstrates that aligning training data with modern research topics combined with reinforcement learning can significantly improve biology reasoning performance.

title: Decomposing Communication Gain and Delay Cost Under Cross-Timestep Delays in Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Communication is essential for coordination in \emph{cooperative} multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability, yet \emph{cross-timestep} delays cause messages to arrive multiple timesteps after generation, inducing temporal misalignment and making information stale when consumed.
  We formalize this setting as a delayed-communication partially observable Markov game (DeComm-POMG) and decompose a message's effect into \emph{communication gain} and \emph{delay cost}, yielding the Communication Gain and Delay Cost (CGDC) metric.
  We further establish a value-loss bound showing that the degradation induced by delayed messages is upper-bounded by a discounted accumulation of an information gap between the action distributions induced by timely versus delayed messages.
  Guided by CGDC, we propose \textbf{CDCMA}, an actor--critic framework that requests messages only when predicted CGDC is positive, predicts future observations to reduce misalignment at consumption, and fuses delayed messages via CGDC-guided attention.
  Experiments on no-teammate-vision variants of Cooperative Navigation and Predator Prey, and on SMAC maps across multiple delay levels show consistent improvements in performance, robustness, and generalization, with ablations validating each component.

AI.tldr: A framework for handling delayed communication in cooperative multi-agent RL that decomposes message effects into gain vs. delay costs, proposes metrics to evaluate this, and introduces CDCMA - an actor-critic method that selectively requests messages and handles delays through observation prediction and attention mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Communication delays in cooperative multi-agent RL cause temporal misalignment between when messages are generated and when they arrive, making information stale and reducing coordination effectiveness. This problem becomes particularly critical in partially observable environments where agents need to coordinate without full information.
AI.method: Formalizes delayed communication as DeComm-POMG (Delayed-Communication Partially Observable Markov Game). Proposes CGDC (Communication Gain and Delay Cost) metric to evaluate message effectiveness. Introduces CDCMA: (1) selectively requests messages only when predicted CGDC is positive, (2) predicts future observations to reduce temporal misalignment, (3) fuses delayed messages using CGDC-guided attention mechanisms.
AI.result: Experiments on Cooperative Navigation, Predator Prey (no-teammate-vision variants), and SMAC maps show consistent improvements across multiple delay levels in performance, robustness, and generalization. Ablation studies validate each component of the proposed CDCMA framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a principled approach to handling delayed communication in cooperative multi-agent RL through formal analysis of delay effects, metrics to evaluate communication utility, and practical methods for selective communication and delay compensation that outperform baseline approaches.

title: Neural Operators for Multi-Task Control and Adaptation
summary: Neural operator methods have emerged as powerful tools for learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, yet their potential in optimal control remains largely unexplored. We focus on multi-task control problems, whose solution is a mapping from task description (e.g., cost or dynamics functions) to optimal control law (e.g., feedback policy). We approximate these solution operators using a permutation-invariant neural operator architecture. Across a range of parametric optimal control environments and a locomotion benchmark, a single operator trained via behavioral cloning accurately approximates the solution operator and generalizes to unseen tasks, out-of-distribution settings, and varying amounts of task observations. We further show that the branch-trunk structure of our neural operator architecture enables efficient and flexible adaptation to new tasks. We develop structured adaptation strategies ranging from lightweight updates to full-network fine-tuning, achieving strong performance across different data and compute settings. Finally, we introduce meta-trained operator variants that optimize the initialization for few-shot adaptation. These methods enable rapid task adaptation with limited data and consistently outperform a popular meta-learning baseline. Together, our results demonstrate that neural operators provide a unified and efficient framework for multi-task control and adaptation.

AI.tldr: Neural operators learn solution mappings from task descriptions to optimal control policies, enabling efficient multi-task control with strong generalization and flexible adaptation.
AI.motivation: While neural operators excel at learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, their application to optimal control problems remains largely unexplored, particularly for multi-task scenarios where solutions map task descriptions (cost/dynamics) to control laws.
AI.method: A permutation-invariant neural operator architecture with branch-trunk structure is used to approximate solution operators. The method includes behavioral cloning training, structured adaptation strategies (lightweight updates to full fine-tuning), and meta-trained variants for few-shot adaptation.
AI.result: A single trained operator accurately approximates solution operators and generalizes to unseen tasks, out-of-distribution settings, and varying task observations. The branch-trunk architecture enables flexible adaptation, with meta-trained variants outperforming meta-learning baselines in rapid task adaptation with limited data.
AI.conclusion: Neural operators provide a unified and efficient framework for multi-task control and adaptation, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities and enabling rapid task adaptation with limited data across diverse control environments.

title: ActionNex: A Virtual Outage Manager for Cloud
summary: Outage management in large-scale cloud operations remains heavily manual, requiring rapid triage, cross-team coordination, and experience-driven decisions under partial observability. We present \textbf{ActionNex}, a production-grade agentic system that supports end-to-end outage assistance, including real-time updates, knowledge distillation, and role- and stage-conditioned next-best action recommendations. ActionNex ingests multimodal operational signals (e.g., outage content, telemetry, and human communications) and compresses them into critical events that represent meaningful state transitions. It couples this perception layer with a hierarchical memory subsystem: long-term Key-Condition-Action (KCA) knowledge distilled from playbooks and historical executions, episodic memory of prior outages, and working memory of the live context. A reasoning agent aligns current critical events to preconditions, retrieves relevant memories, and generates actionable recommendations; executed human actions serve as an implicit feedback signal to enable continual self-evolution in a human-agent hybrid system. We evaluate ActionNex on eight real Azure outages (8M tokens, 4,000 critical events) using two complementary ground-truth action sets, achieving 71.4\% precision and 52.8-54.8\% recall. The system has been piloted in production and has received positive early feedback.

AI.tldr: ActionNex is an AI agentic system for automating outage management in cloud operations that ingests multimodal signals, uses hierarchical memory, and provides actionable recommendations with 71.4% precision and 52.8-54.8% recall.
AI.motivation: Outage management in large-scale cloud operations is heavily manual, requiring rapid triage, cross-team coordination, and experience-driven decisions under partial observability, which is inefficient and error-prone.
AI.method: ActionNex uses multimodal signal ingestion (outage content, telemetry, human communications) compressed into critical events, coupled with hierarchical memory (long-term KCA knowledge, episodic memory, working memory) and a reasoning agent that aligns events to preconditions and generates recommendations.
AI.result: Evaluation on eight real Azure outages (8M tokens, 4,000 critical events) showed 71.4% precision and 52.8-54.8% recall using two complementary ground-truth action sets. The system has been piloted in production with positive early feedback.
AI.conclusion: ActionNex demonstrates a production-grade agentic system for outage management that can provide effective recommendations while enabling continual self-evolution through human-agent collaboration in hybrid systems.

title: Representational Collapse in Multi-Agent LLM Committees: Measurement and Diversity-Aware Consensus
summary: Multi-agent LLM committees replicate the same model under different role prompts and aggregate outputs by majority vote, implicitly assuming that agents contribute complementary evidence. We embed each agent's chain-of-thought rationale and measure pairwise similarity: across 100 GSM8K questions with three Qwen2.5-14B agents, mean cosine similarity is 0.888 and effective rank is 2.17 out of 3.0, a failure mode we term representational collapse. DALC, a training-free consensus protocol that computes diversity weights from embedding geometry, reaches 87% on GSM8K versus 84% for self-consistency at 26% lower token cost. Ablation experiments reveal 1-3 point per-protocol run-to-run variance, confirm that hint sharing contributes more than diversity weighting alone, and show that encoder choice strongly modulates collapse severity (cosine 0.908 with mxbai versus 0.888 with nomic) and downstream accuracy. The more robust finding is that collapse is measurable, worsens on harder tasks, and that the choice of embedding proxy is a first-order design decision for any latent communication protocol.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent LLM committees suffer from "representational collapse" where agents generate similar rationales despite role prompts, reducing diversity benefits; a training-free protocol (DALC) improves performance by computing diversity weights from embedding geometry.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM committees typically assume that different role prompts lead to complementary evidence and diverse reasoning, but there's a need to verify this assumption and address potential similarity in agent outputs that reduces the benefits of ensemble methods.
AI.method: The authors measure pairwise similarity between agents' chain-of-thought rationales using cosine similarity and effective rank metrics. They propose DALC (Diversity-Aware Latent Consensus), a training-free protocol that computes diversity weights from embedding geometry. Experiments use three Qwen2.5-14B agents on GSM8K with different embedding encoders (mxbai vs nomic).
AI.result: Agents show high similarity (mean cosine 0.888, effective rank 2.17/3.0), indicating representational collapse. DALC achieves 87% accuracy vs 84% for self-consistency with 26% lower token cost. Embedding choice strongly affects collapse severity (0.908 with mxbai vs 0.888 with nomic). Ablations show 1-3 point variance, hint sharing contributes more than diversity weighting alone, and collapse worsens on harder tasks.
AI.conclusion: Representational collapse is measurable and reduces ensemble effectiveness. The choice of embedding proxy is crucial for latent communication protocols. DALC effectively addresses collapse by leveraging diversity-aware weighting, showing that embedding geometry can be used to improve multi-agent performance without training.

title: Earth Embeddings Reveal Diverse Urban Signals from Space
summary: Conventional urban indicators derived from censuses, surveys, and administrative records are often costly, spatially inconsistent, and slow to update. Recent geospatial foundation models enable Earth embeddings, compact satellite image representations transferable across downstream tasks, but their utility for neighborhood-scale urban monitoring remains unclear. Here, we benchmark three Earth embedding families, AlphaEarth, Prithvi, and Clay, for urban signal prediction across six U.S. metropolitan areas from 2020 to 2023. Using a unified supervised-learning framework, we predict 14 neighborhood-level indicators spanning crime, income, health, and travel behavior, and evaluate performance under four settings: global, city-wise, year-wise, and city-year. Results show that Earth embeddings capture substantial urban variation, with the highest predictive skill for outcomes more directly tied to built-environment structure, including chronic health burdens and dominant commuting modes. By contrast, indicators shaped more strongly by fine-scale behavior and local policy, such as cycling, remain difficult to infer. Predictive performance varies markedly across cities but remains comparatively stable across years, indicating strong spatial heterogeneity alongside temporal robustness. Exploratory analysis suggests that cross-city variation in predictive performance is associated with urban form in task-specific ways. Controlled dimensionality experiments show that representation efficiency is critical: compact 64-dimensional AlphaEarth embeddings remain more informative than 64-dimensional reductions of Prithvi and Clay. This study establishes a benchmark for evaluating Earth embeddings in urban remote sensing and demonstrates their potential as scalable, low-cost features for SDG-aligned neighborhood-scale urban monitoring.

AI.tldr: Earth embeddings from satellite imagery show promise for neighborhood-scale urban monitoring, with best performance for built-environment outcomes and stable temporal performance across years.
AI.motivation: Conventional urban indicators from censuses and surveys are costly, spatially inconsistent, and slow to update. The study investigates whether geospatial foundation models that create Earth embeddings from satellite imagery can provide scalable, low-cost alternatives for urban monitoring at neighborhood scales.
AI.method: Benchmarked three Earth embedding families (AlphaEarth, Prithvi, Clay) across six U.S. metropolitan areas from 2020-2023 using a unified supervised-learning framework. Predicted 14 neighborhood-level indicators spanning crime, income, health, and travel behavior, evaluating performance under four settings: global, city-wise, year-wise, and city-year.
AI.result: Earth embeddings capture substantial urban variation with highest predictive skill for outcomes tied to built-environment structure (chronic health burdens, dominant commuting modes). Performance varies across cities but remains stable across years. Cross-city performance variation is associated with urban form. Compact 64-dimensional AlphaEarth embeddings outperformed dimensionally-reduced versions of other embeddings.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a benchmark for evaluating Earth embeddings in urban remote sensing and demonstrates their potential as scalable, low-cost features for SDG-aligned neighborhood-scale urban monitoring, particularly for indicators related to built environment structure.

title: Structural Rigidity and the 57-Token Predictive Window: A Physical Framework for Inference-Layer Governability in Large Language Models
comment: Extends arXiv:2603.21415. 30 pages. Also available on Zenodo (10.5281/zenodo.19393882)
summary:
Current AI safety relies on behavioral monitoring and post-training alignment, yet empirical measurement shows these approaches produce no detectable pre-commitment signal in a majority of instruction-tuned models tested. We present an energy-based governance framework connecting transformer inference dynamics to constraint-satisfaction models of neural computation, and apply it to a seven-model cohort across five geometric regimes.
  Using trajectory tension (rho = ||a|| / ||v||), we identify a 57-token pre-commitment window in Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct under greedy decoding on arithmetic constraint probes. This result is model-specific, task-specific, and configuration-specific, demonstrating that pre-commitment signals can exist but are not universal.
  We introduce a five-regime taxonomy of inference behavior: Authority Band, Late Signal, Inverted, Flat, and Scaffold-Selective. Energy asymmetry (Σ\r{ho}_misaligned / Σ\r{ho}_aligned) serves as a unifying metric of structural rigidity across these regimes.
  Across seven models, only one configuration exhibits a predictive signal prior to commitment; all others show silent failure, late detection, inverted dynamics, or flat geometry.
  We further demonstrate that factual hallucination produces no predictive signal across 72 test conditions, consistent with spurious attractor settling in the absence of a trained world-model constraint.
  These results establish that rule violation and hallucination are distinct failure modes with different detection requirements. Internal geometry monitoring is effective only where resistance exists; detection of factual confabulation requires external verification mechanisms.
  This work provides a measurable framework for inference-layer governability and introduces a taxonomy for evaluating deployment risk in autonomous AI systems.

AI.tldr: Behavioral monitoring in AI safety often fails to detect pre-commitment signals before models violate constraints. The paper introduces an energy-based governance framework that reveals only one model configuration out of seven tested shows predictive signals before rule violations, establishing distinct failure modes for different types of AI errors.
AI.motivation: Current AI safety approaches rely on behavioral monitoring and post-training alignment, but empirical measurements show these produce no detectable pre-commitment signals in most instruction-tuned models tested. There's a need to understand when and how AI systems commit to violating constraints before they execute those violations.
AI.method: The authors present an energy-based governance framework connecting transformer inference dynamics to constraint-satisfaction models of neural computation. They use trajectory tension (rho = ||a||/||v||) to measure model behavior across seven models in five geometric regimes, specifically testing arithmetic constraint probes with greedy decoding.
AI.result: Only one model configuration (Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct) exhibited a 57-token pre-commitment window among seven models tested. The paper identifies five distinct regimes of inference behavior and shows factual hallucination produces no predictive signal across 72 test conditions, indicating rule violation and hallucination are distinct failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Rule violation and hallucination require different detection approaches. Internal geometry monitoring only works where resistance exists, while factual confabulation detection requires external verification. The work provides a measurable framework for inference-layer governability and a taxonomy for evaluating deployment risk in autonomous AI systems.

title: PolySwarm: A Multi-Agent Large Language Model Framework for Prediction Market Trading and Latency Arbitrage
summary: This paper presents PolySwarm, a novel multi-agent large language model (LLM) framework designed for real-time prediction market trading and latency arbitrage on decentralized platforms such as Polymarket. PolySwarm deploys a swarm of 50 diverse LLM personas that concurrently evaluate binary outcome markets, aggregating individual probability estimates through confidence-weighted Bayesian combination of swarm consensus with market-implied probabilities, and applying quarter-Kelly position sizing for risk-controlled execution. The system incorporates an information-theoretic market analysis engine using Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence and Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence to detect cross-market inefficiencies and negation pair mispricings. A latency arbitrage module exploits stale Polymarket prices by deriving CEX-implied probabilities from a log-normal pricing model and executing trades within the human reaction-time window. We provide a full architectural description, implementation details, and evaluation methodology using Brier scores, calibration analysis, and log-loss metrics benchmarked against human superforecaster performance. We further discuss open challenges including hallucination in agent pools, computational cost at scale, regulatory exposure, and feedback-loop risk, and outline five priority directions for future research. Experimental results demonstrate that swarm aggregation consistently outperforms single-model baselines in probability calibration on Polymarket prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: PolySwarm: A multi-agent LLM framework for real-time prediction market trading and latency arbitrage on Polymarket, using 50 diverse LLM personas with Bayesian aggregation and risk-controlled execution.
AI.motivation: To address the need for sophisticated, automated trading systems on decentralized prediction markets like Polymarket that can leverage LLMs for probabilistic forecasting while managing risk and exploiting market inefficiencies.
AI.method: Deploys 50 diverse LLM personas to evaluate binary outcome markets, uses confidence-weighted Bayesian combination of swarm consensus with market-implied probabilities, implements quarter-Kelly position sizing, and incorporates information-theoretic analysis (KL & JS divergence) for cross-market inefficiencies plus latency arbitrage exploiting stale Polymarket prices.
AI.result: Swarm aggregation consistently outperforms single-model baselines in probability calibration on Polymarket prediction tasks, evaluated using Brier scores, calibration analysis, and log-loss metrics benchmarked against human superforecaster performance.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent LLM framework effectively improves forecasting accuracy in decentralized prediction markets, though challenges remain including hallucination in agent pools, computational cost, regulatory exposure, and feedback-loop risks, with five priority directions identified for future research.

title: Super Agents and Confounders: Influence of surrounding agents on vehicle trajectory prediction
summary: In highly interactive driving scenes, trajectory prediction is conditioned on information from surrounding traffic participants such as cars and pedestrians. Our main contribution is a comprehensive analysis of state-of-the-art trajectory predictors, which reveals a surprising and critical flaw: many surrounding agents degrade prediction accuracy rather than improve it. Using Shapley-based attribution, we rigorously demonstrate that models learn unstable and non-causal decision-making schemes that vary significantly across training runs. Building on these insights, we propose to integrate a Conditional Information Bottleneck (CIB), which does not require additional supervision and is trained to effectively compress agent features as well as ignore those that are not beneficial for the prediction task. Comprehensive experiments using multiple datasets and model architectures demonstrate that this simple yet effective approach not only improves overall trajectory prediction performance in many cases but also increases robustness to different perturbations. Our results highlight the importance of selectively integrating contextual information, which can often contain spurious or misleading signals, in trajectory prediction. Moreover, we provide interpretable metrics for identifying non-robust behavior and present a promising avenue towards a solution.

AI.tldr: Surrounding agents in trajectory prediction often degrade accuracy; authors propose Conditional Information Bottleneck to selectively compress beneficial features and ignore misleading signals.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a critical flaw in trajectory prediction: contrary to intuition, using information from surrounding agents often decreases prediction accuracy rather than improving it, suggesting models learn unstable and non-causal decision patterns.
AI.method: Using Shapley-based attribution to analyze model decisions, then proposing Conditional Information Bottleneck (CIB) without additional supervision to compress beneficial agent features while ignoring harmful ones.
AI.result: CIB improves trajectory prediction performance across multiple datasets and architectures, increases robustness to perturbations, and provides interpretable metrics for identifying non-robust behavior.
AI.conclusion: Selective integration of contextual information is crucial for trajectory prediction as surrounding agents often contain spurious signals; CIB provides an effective solution without extra supervision.

title: Explainable Model Routing for Agentic Workflows
comment: ACM CHI 2026 Human-Centered Explainable AI (HCXAI) Workshop (Spotlight)
summary: Modern agentic workflows decompose complex tasks into specialized subtasks and route them to diverse models to minimize cost without sacrificing quality. However, current routing architectures focus exclusively on performance optimization, leaving underlying trade-offs between model capability and cost unrecorded. Without clear rationale, developers cannot distinguish between intelligent efficiency -- using specialized models for appropriate tasks -- and latent failures caused by budget-driven model selection. We present Topaz, a framework that introduces formal auditability to agentic routing. Topaz replaces silent model assignments with an inherently interpretable router that incorporates three components: (i) skill-based profiling that synthesizes performance across diverse benchmarks into granular capability profiles (ii) fully traceable routing algorithms that utilize budget-based and multi-objective optimization to produce clear traces of how skill-match scores were weighed against costs, and (iii) developer-facing explanations that translate these traces into natural language, allowing users to audit system logic and iteratively tune the cost-quality tradeoff. By making routing decisions interpretable, Topaz enables users to understand, trust, and meaningfully steer routed agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Topaz is a framework that makes agentic workflow routing interpretable by adding formal auditability, allowing developers to understand and trust model selection decisions.
AI.motivation: Current routing architectures focus only on performance optimization without recording the trade-offs between model capability and cost, making it impossible for developers to distinguish intelligent efficiency from budget-driven failures.
AI.method: Topaz introduces an interpretable router with three components: skill-based profiling that creates granular capability profiles from diverse benchmarks, fully traceable routing algorithms using budget-based and multi-objective optimization, and developer-facing natural language explanations.
AI.result: Topaz enables users to audit system logic, understand routing decisions, and iteratively tune cost-quality tradeoffs, making routed agentic systems more transparent and trustworthy.
AI.conclusion: By making routing decisions interpretable, Topaz allows developers to understand, trust, and meaningfully steer agentic workflows, addressing the critical gap in current routing architectures that lack auditability.

title: Three Phases of Expert Routing: How Load Balance Evolves During Mixture-of-Experts Training
summary: We model Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) token routing as a congestion game with a single effective parameter, the congestion coefficient gamma_eff, that quantifies the balance-quality tradeoff. Tracking gamma_eff across training checkpoints of two open-source MoE models, OLMoE-1B-7B (20 checkpoints, with dense sampling in the surge region) and OpenMoE-8B (6 checkpoints), reveals a three-phase trajectory: a surge phase where the router learns to balance load (gamma_eff: 14 to 36-39, peaking in the step 30K-40K region), a stabilization phase where experts specialize under steady balance (B_0: 2.4 to 2.3, steps 100K-400K), and a relaxation phase where the router trades balance for quality as experts differentiate (gamma_eff: 27 to 9, steps 400K-1.2M). This non-monotone trajectory, invisible to post-hoc analysis of converged models, reveals that early MoE training prioritizes balance while late training prioritizes quality. The theoretical framework is honest about its limits: the single-type equilibrium reduces to temperature-scaled softmax (held-out L1: MFG = 0.199 vs. softmax = 0.200). The game is not a better predictor; it reveals what the temperature means and, critically, how that temperature evolves. We complement the dynamics with an effective congestion decomposition, a multi-type extension that improves load prediction via token clustering on all 16 layers (mean: 30%), scope diagnostics (K/M, epsilon_l), and robustness verification across four independent quality estimators (r >= 0.89). All confidence intervals are from bootstrap resampling over 50 independent text batches.

AI.tldr: MoE token routing is modeled as a congestion game revealing a three-phase training trajectory: early surge (balance), stabilization (specialization), and late relaxation (quality over balance). The framework exposes training dynamics invisible in converged models.
AI.motivation: To understand the tradeoff between load balancing and routing quality in Mixture-of-Experts models during training, which post-hoc analysis of converged models cannot reveal.
AI.method: Model MoE token routing as congestion game with congestion coefficient gamma_eff; track across training checkpoints of OLMoE-1B-7B (20 checkpoints) and OpenMoE-8B (6 checkpoints); complement with effective congestion decomposition, multi-type extension via token clustering, and robustness verification across four quality estimators.
AI.result: Three-phase training trajectory identified: surge phase (gamma_eff: 14→36-39, peak at steps 30K-40K), stabilization phase (B_0: 2.4→2.3, steps 100K-400K), relaxation phase (gamma_eff: 27→9, steps 400K-1.2M). Early training prioritizes balance, late training prioritizes quality. Multi-type extension improves load prediction by 30% across all 16 layers.
AI.conclusion: The congestion game framework reveals crucial training dynamics of MoE models - that routing evolves non-monotonically from prioritizing load balance early to favoring routing quality later. While not a better predictor than temperature-scaled softmax, it provides interpretability for routing temperature evolution during training.

title: Investigating Data Interventions for Subgroup Fairness: An ICU Case Study
summary: In high-stakes settings where machine learning models are used to automate decision-making about individuals, the presence of algorithmic bias can exacerbate systemic harm to certain subgroups of people. These biases often stem from the underlying training data. In practice, interventions to "fix the data" depend on the actual additional data sources available -- where many are less than ideal. In these cases, the effects of data scaling on subgroup performance become volatile, as the improvements from increased sample size are counteracted by the introduction of distribution shifts in the training set. In this paper, we investigate the limitations of combining data sources to improve subgroup performance within the context of healthcare. Clinical models are commonly trained on datasets comprised of patient electronic health record (EHR) data from different hospitals or admission departments. Across two such datasets, the eICU Collaborative Research Database and the MIMIC-IV dataset, we find that data addition can both help and hurt model fairness and performance, and many intuitive strategies for data selection are unreliable. We compare model-based post-hoc calibration and data-centric addition strategies to find that the combination of both is important to improve subgroup performance. Our work questions the traditional dogma of "better data" for overcoming fairness challenges by comparing and combining data- and model-based approaches.

AI.tldr: Data addition can both help and hurt fairness in healthcare ML models, requiring combined data and model approaches rather than just "better data" dogma.
AI.motivation: Algorithmic bias in high-stakes ML decision-making can harm subgroups, often due to training data issues. In healthcare, interventions to fix data face challenges with available data sources, making data scaling effects volatile - improvements from larger samples may be counteracted by distribution shifts.
AI.method: Investigated limitations of combining data sources to improve subgroup performance in healthcare using clinical models trained on patient EHR data from different hospitals. Used eICU Collaborative Research Database and MIMIC-IV datasets, comparing data-centric addition strategies with model-based post-hoc calibration approaches.
AI.result: Found that data addition can both help and hurt model fairness and performance, with intuitive data selection strategies proving unreliable. The combination of both model-based post-hoc calibration and data-centric addition strategies was important for improving subgroup performance.
AI.conclusion: Questions the traditional dogma of "better data" alone for overcoming fairness challenges, advocating for comparing and combining both data- and model-based approaches to address algorithmic bias in healthcare settings.

title: Automated Analysis of Global AI Safety Initiatives: A Taxonomy-Driven LLM Approach
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures, 6 tables, to be published in PoliticalNLP 2026
summary: We present an automated crosswalk framework that compares an AI safety policy document pair under a shared taxonomy of activities. Using the activity categories defined in Activity Map on AI Safety as fixed aspects, the system extracts and maps relevant activities, then produces for each aspect a short summary for each document, a brief comparison, and a similarity score. We assess the stability and validity of LLM-based crosswalk analysis across public policy documents. Using five large language models, we perform crosswalks on ten publicly available documents and visualize mean similarity scores with a heatmap. The results show that model choice substantially affects the crosswalk outcomes, and that some document pairs yield high disagreements across models. A human evaluation by three experts on two document pairs shows high inter-annotator agreement, while model scores still differ from human judgments. These findings support comparative inspection of policy documents.

AI.tldr: Automated framework compares AI safety policy documents using LLMs to extract, map, and score activities under a shared taxonomy, revealing model-dependent results and differences from human judgments.
AI.motivation: To enable systematic comparison of AI safety policy documents under a common framework and assess how different language models perform in this crosswalk analysis task.
AI.method: Developed automated crosswalk framework using Activity Map on AI Safety taxonomy; extracted and mapped activities from document pairs; used five LLMs to generate similarity scores; conducted human evaluation with three experts on two document pairs.
AI.result: Model choice substantially affects crosswalk outcomes; some document pairs yield high disagreements across models; human experts show high inter-annotator agreement but model scores differ from human judgments; visualized results with similarity score heatmap.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports comparative inspection of policy documents but highlights the importance of model selection and shows current limitations in matching human judgments.

title: Soft Tournament Equilibrium
summary: The evaluation of general-purpose artificial agents, particularly those based on large language models, presents a significant challenge due to the non-transitive nature of their interactions. When agent A defeats B, B defeats C, and C defeats A, traditional ranking methods that force a linear ordering can be misleading and unstable. We argue that for such cyclic domains, the fundamental object of evaluation should not be a ranking but a set-valued core, as conceptualized in classical tournament theory. This paper introduces Soft Tournament Equilibrium (STE), a differentiable framework for learning and computing set-valued tournament solutions directly from pairwise comparison data. STE first learns a probabilistic tournament model, potentially conditioned on rich contextual information. It then employs novel, differentiable operators for soft reachability and soft covering to compute continuous analogues of two seminal tournament solutions: the Top Cycle and the Uncovered Set. The output is a set of core agents, each with a calibrated membership score, providing a nuanced and robust assessment of agent capabilities. We develop the theoretical foundation for STE to prove its consistency with classical solutions in the zero-temperature limit, which establishes its Condorcet-inclusion properties, and analyzing its stability and sample complexity. We specify an experimental protocol for validating STE on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks. This work aims to provide a complete, standalone treatise that re-centers general-agent evaluation on a more appropriate and robust theoretical foundation, moving from unstable rankings to stable, set-valued equilibria.

AI.tldr: STE (Soft Tournament Equilibrium) is a differentiable framework that learns set-valued tournament solutions from pairwise comparison data to evaluate AI agents with non-transitive interactions, moving from unstable rankings to stable core sets.
AI.motivation: Traditional ranking methods for evaluating AI agents (especially LLM-based ones) are misleading and unstable when agent interactions exhibit non-transitive cycles (A beats B, B beats C, C beats A). Linear rankings can't capture these complex dynamics, requiring a more appropriate theoretical foundation.
AI.method: 1) Learns a probabilistic tournament model from pairwise comparison data, potentially with contextual conditioning. 2) Uses novel differentiable operators for soft reachability and soft covering. 3) Computes continuous analogues of classical tournament solutions: Top Cycle and Uncovered Set. 4) Outputs core agents with calibrated membership scores.
AI.result: Provides a complete framework for robust agent evaluation that produces set-valued tournament solutions (core agents) rather than linear rankings. Proves theoretical consistency with classical solutions in zero-temperature limit (Condorcet-inclusion properties), analyzes stability and sample complexity, and establishes experimental validation protocol.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a fundamental shift in AI agent evaluation from unstable linear rankings to stable set-valued equilibria based on tournament theory, providing a more appropriate and robust theoretical foundation for assessing agents with non-transitive interactions.

title: Improving Feasibility via Fast Autoencoder-Based Projections
summary: Enforcing complex (e.g., nonconvex) operational constraints is a critical challenge in real-world learning and control systems. However, existing methods struggle to efficiently enforce general classes of constraints. To address this, we propose a novel data-driven amortized approach that uses a trained autoencoder as an approximate projector to provide fast corrections to infeasible predictions. Specifically, we train an autoencoder using an adversarial objective to learn a structured, convex latent representation of the feasible set.
